name: Deep Dive: Java List Interface
chatResponse: I'll break down the List interface by comparing its primary implementations and explaining when to choose one over the other based on performance and memory constraints.

steps.title: The List Contract
steps.content:
The `List` interface extends `Collection` and represents an **ordered sequence** of elements (also known as a sequence). Key features include:
- **Positional Access:** You can manipulate elements based on their numerical index.
- **Duplicates:** Unlike Sets, Lists allow multiple identical elements.
- **Ordering:** They maintain the insertion order of elements.

steps.title: ArrayList: The Go-To Implementation
steps.content:
Under the hood, `ArrayList` uses a **dynamic array**. 
- **Access:** $O(1)$ time to get an element by index.
- **Resizing:** When the array is full, it creates a new, larger array (usually 1.5x size) and copies elements. This makes additions **amortized** $O(1)$.
- **Efficiency:** It's the most memory-efficient for storing elements but expensive for insertions/deletions in the middle ($O(n)$) because elements must be shifted.

steps.title: LinkedList: The Doubly-Linked Alternative
steps.content:
Each element in a `LinkedList` is stored in a **Node** that contains a reference to the previous and next nodes.
- **Access:** $O(n)$ time to find an element (must traverse from the head or tail).
- **Insert/Delete:** $O(1)$ if you are already at the node (e.g., adding to the start or end).
- **Memory:** Higher overhead than `ArrayList` because of the extra pointers stored in every node.

steps.title: Legacy and Thread-Safe Variants
steps.content:
- **Vector:** Similar to `ArrayList` but **synchronized** (thread-safe). It's generally slower due to locking overhead.
- **Stack:** A subclass of `Vector` that follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out). Modern Java suggests using `Deque` (e.g., `ArrayDeque`) instead of `Stack`.
- **CopyOnWriteArrayList:** A thread-safe variant used in concurrent programming where every 'mutation' creates a fresh copy of the underlying array.

visualCode:
List Implementation Comparison

Feature ArrayList LinkedList Vector
Data Structure Dynamic Array Doubly Linked List Dynamic Array
Get (by Index) O(1) - Fast O(n) - Slow O(1) - Fast
Insert/Delete (Ends) O(1) Amortized O(1) O(1) Amortized
Insert/Delete (Middle) O(n) - Shift elements O(n) - Traverse nodes O(n) - Shift elements
Thread Safe No No Yes (Synchronized)
Memory Overhead Low High (Nodes/Pointers) Low
